Poverty Analysis — Sedgwick County, KS

Demo analysis · spatial-machines · 2026-04-16

The headline. In Sedgwick County (Wichita and surrounding area), roughly 70,351 residents live below the federal poverty threshold — 13.7% of the county's 514,863-person poverty universe. That rate is nearly evenly split above and below the county mean (15.5%), but the spatial distribution tells a sharper story: the top 10 highest-poverty tracts cover just 5% of the county's geographic footprint. Poverty here is concentrated, not diffuse.

Three tracts driving the upper tail

Ranked by poverty rate. These three alone account for 4,175 residents in poverty — roughly 5.9% of the county total, from 8,671 residents in the combined poverty universe.

Statistical charts

The histogram reveals skew hidden by the map's color classes; the lollipop chart names the tracts where poverty concentrates. Tract Census Tract 78 has the highest rate at 54.0%.

Interactive map

Click any tract for its poverty rate, resident counts, and GEOID. Toggle between the poverty-rate and below-poverty-count layers in the upper right.

Open interactive map in new tab

How to read this

Natural-breaks classification groups tracts so that within-class variance is minimized — visually, classes separate where the distribution has natural gaps. The upper classes on this map aren't "slightly worse than the middle"; they're meaningfully disconnected from it. That's what the paired histogram shows (a long right tail), and it's what makes the three tracts called out above worth individual attention in a real engagement.

Caveats — read before citing

  • ACS margins of error. These estimates come from the 5-year American Community Survey sample, not a full count. Small tracts can have wide confidence intervals; the pipeline flags tracts with CV > 0.15 when running the full statistics stage.
  • Poverty universe vs. total population. The denominator here is "population for whom poverty status is determined" — it excludes residents of group quarters (nursing homes, prisons, dorms). A tract dominated by one of those institutions can look artificially low.
  • This is a demo. Real engagements would add MOE maps, a significant-hotspot analysis (Getis-Ord Gi* with FDR correction), and a peer-review pass before shipping — all supported by the pipeline but out of scope for a 20-second reproducible demo.

What this demo is showing you

Everything on this page came out of one command (make demo or python demo.py) on bundled Census data, with no API keys and no internet. Deliberately minimal: one variable, one county, one map, two charts, one interactive layer, one report.

The full pipeline, given an AI coding agent and a real question, does much more: retrieval from 20+ data sources, join + CRS standardization, MOE propagation, significant-hotspot analysis (Getis-Ord Gi* with FDR correction), spatial autocorrelation (Moran's I / LISA), independent validation, peer review, and a solution graph — a DAG capturing every input, operation, and output across all 9 agents and their intermediate handoffs. The solution graph from a full run is dense and genuinely useful; the one this demo would produce is thin enough to be more confusing than helpful, so it's not shown here. See the project README for example prompts that exercise the real pipeline.

Generated by spatial-machines · Data: U.S. Census Bureau ACS 5-Year Estimates · Outputs in analyses\demo-sedgwick-poverty/outputs/
